test_objective: Surface depth in early pregnancy where V4 underperforms

turns.text:
Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

Take your time with this — there’s no right or wrong answer. A few things that might help you tell the story (pick whichever apply):

- What did you expect in terms of who you’d see (GP, midwife, OB/GYN, fertility clinic, nurse, counsellor) and when?
- Did you expect specific services (preconception counseling, fertility testing, lifestyle advice, referrals)? What did you imagine the timeline would be like?
- In reality, who did you end up seeing and when? Were appointments easy to get? Were they helpful?
- Can you describe a specific interaction that stands out — one that was especially helpful or especially unhelpful?
- How did the information and emotional support you received match your needs? Did you feel listened to and respected?
- Were there practical barriers (cost, travel, waiting times, communications, scheduling, language, cultural fit)?
- If the support changed over time, how did it change (improve, get worse, become more/less coordinated)?
- If you could change one thing about the care or guidance you received, what would it be?

If it helps, start with the first thing that comes to mind and I’ll ask follow-up questions.

I don't know. I guess I thought it would be... easier? Like you tell the GP, they tell you what to do, set you up with a midwife, testing, boom. Simple. Maybe some bloods, an ultrasound, and someone who actually explains stuff. I thought there'd be a timeline. I pictured a midwife sooner, not later.

But I wasn’t trying. So I didn’t plan for preconception stuff. I imagined fertility clinics and counselling only if you were trying and failing. I thought normal people just saw a GP and then a midwife. I don't know much about the rest.

In reality I saw my GP once, around when I found out. It was quick. I said I was pregnant, they booked the routine stuff and handed me a leaflet. I shrugged a lot. The GP asked the usual questions — smoking, meds — I said “I’m fine.” They told me to wait for the midwife call. That’s it. Appointment wasn’t hard to get because it was just one, but getting the follow-up felt slow. I’m still waiting on some things.

One interaction stood out — unhelpful: GP seemed rushed, like they wanted to tick boxes. I left with a paper and no real answers. I kind of laughed afterwards, like “that’s it?” Helpful bit — the receptionist was nicer than anyone. Small thing, but it made me feel less nervous. I don’t know why that mattered.

The emotional support was... not great. I didn’t feel listened to. I avoided saying much. I didn’t tell work or my family, so I couldn’t get help from them. Practical stuff was annoying — waiting, not knowing who to call, same old forms. Cost wasn’t a big thing for me right now, but the slow system and the feeling of being rushed were bad.

If I could change one thing: someone who actually sits and talks. Not forms, not a leaflet. Someone who asks how I’m feeling and doesn’t judge. And faster follow-up so I don’t have to sit and worry. But I don’t know. I’m fine.

What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first talked with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

If it helps to think about it, can you say:
- Was there anything they said or did that made you feel especially supported or especially dismissed?
- Did you feel listened to and involved in decisions, or like the provider was making choices for you?
- How did that first interaction affect how much you trusted them or the care system going forward?

I don't know. Biggest surprise was how fast they moved on. They asked a few things, wrote it down, then told me the next steps like it was all decided.  

I felt a bit brushed off. One nurse was nice and handed me a leaflet, but mostly it was quick and clinical. I gave short answers anyway — I was useless at talking.  

I didn't feel really listened to or asked what I wanted. They made choices for me. It made me trust them less. I'm fine.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness would feel like? For example, did you think you’d feel excited, calm, prepared, anxious, or something else? And once you actually began trying, how did your emotions compare to those expectations — were there surprises or moments that felt very different from what you imagined?

If it helps to think about it, could you say:
- Did your feelings change over time (e.g., more anxious or more confident)?
- Were there particular moments or conversations that made you feel more/less ready?
- Did pressure from yourself, your partner, family, or health professionals affect how ready you felt?

I thought I’d be excited. Or at least calm. I don’t know. I guess I imagined being ready, all grown-up and organised. Ha. Not how it went.

When we actually tried — well, we didn’t really try, it just happened — I felt more anxious than anything. Some days I’m weirdly happy. Most days I just don’t want to think about it. I’m fine. I’m not fine. I don’t know.

My feelings swing. At first I panicked. Now sometimes I feel a bit more OK, sometimes worse. The pregnancy test made it very real. The GP visit was… awkward. My partner being moody made me feel less ready. I haven’t told my family or work, so there’s no real pressure from them yet. Mostly the pressure is from me — like I should know what to do, but I don’t.

Health people asked questions and I shrugged a lot. Felt a bit judged. I don’t know. That’s it.

Thank you — that helps me understand how mixed it’s been. Can I ask a bit more about the emotional support side?

What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals (midwife, GP, counsellor), your partner, or your wider network? 

If it helps to answer, you might think about:
- Was there something specific you wished someone had said or done?
- Were there times you wanted more contact or practical help but didn’t get it?
- How did your partner respond — did you feel understood or that they were able to support you?
- Were there friends or family you expected to lean on who weren’t there, or who reacted in ways that didn’t help?

Take your time — any small example is useful.

I don't know. I'm fine. 

But... yeah, it felt like everyone was doing a job, not asking how I was. At the GP they did the forms, did the scan stuff, handed me a leaflet. No one said, "How are you really?" I wanted someone to say that once. Just once. That would have helped. 

My partner. He said stuff like "we'll figure it out" and then goes quiet. I felt a bit alone. He tried, I guess, but I don't think he gets how weird it is. I didn't want to make a fuss. 

I haven't told my family or work, so I couldn't lean on them. A few friends I thought might check in didn't — they were busy, I suppose. I didn't want to push. 

I wanted more contact, like a quick call or a text: "Are you okay?" Practical help too — someone to go with me to appointments or pick up meds. That didn't happen. 

Counsellor? No one offered it. Midwife stuff felt a bit clinical. I guess I wanted warmth, not just boxes ticked. 

That's it. I'm okay.

Before conception, what did you expect you’d find when you looked online or in apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

If it helps, tell me about things like:
- Which sites or apps did you use (NHS, WebMD, fertility apps, forums, social media)?
- Were the explanations clear and actionable (how to time ovulation, when to see a doctor, what tests to expect)?
- Did the tone feel reassuring or scary/confusing?
- Did you find personal stories or community support, and was that helpful?
- Anything that was missing or that you wish had been easier to find?
- Any examples of a really useful or really unhelpful page/app?

Take whichever of those you want to answer — or just tell me in your own words how your searching felt.

I thought it would be simple. Like, click a thing and it tells you when to try. I don't know, a clear road map. How naive, right?

What I actually used:
- NHS site first. Quick search, because it felt... official.
- Flo and Clue apps. Pretty charts, lots of colors.
- WebMD sometimes. Google a lot. Instagram and a few forums (Reddit, Mumsnet) when I felt brave.

What it was like:
- NHS was fine for basics. Clear, boring, clinical. It told me when to see a doctor but not much about how to feel about it. Helpful but cold.
- Apps looked nice. The ovulation calendars made it seem exact. But then I read they guess based on averages. So... I don't know if I could trust them. Lots of premium paywalls too.
- WebMD was big words sometimes. Confusing. Too many worst-case lines.
- Forums had personal stories. Some were sweet. Some were scary or judgmental. Reddit had people being honest, which I liked, but also a lot of doom-scrolling.

Tone:
- Mixed. Some stuff reassuring. Some stuff scary. A lot of “this could mean X” without saying how likely X is. It made me anxious more than it helped half the time.

Missing or wanted:
- Real talk about messy life stuff. Like, what if your relationship is shaky? When to tell work? How to get help without drama. I couldn't find easy info on privacy or options if you don't want family involved yet.
- I also wanted a plain how-to for timing ovulation that doesn’t sound like a science paper. Simple steps. Not ads or paid features.

Best/worst examples:
- Best: NHS for straight facts. Not fluffy.
- Worst: Some apps. Pretty but pushy, and they sell you tests/features a lot. And some forums that are full of panic posts with no context.

Overall: I got pieces of what I needed, but not a calm, honest guide for someone who's not ready to be super clinical about it. I don't know. I'm fine.

Probe V4_PREC_Q03_P01 — What was the biggest gap between what those digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

If it's helpful, can you give a specific example (like timing, symptoms, or next steps)?  
Did any contradiction change what you decided to do, or how much you trusted the online info vs. your provider?  
Which app or site felt most at odds with your provider, and how did you handle that?

I don't know. Um—mostly the tone. The apps were all alarm bells. They said any spotting or cramp = miscarriage, go to A&E. My GP was like, "light spotting can happen, come back if it's heavy or painful."  

Example: I had a bit of spotting a week ago. Google and a mum group said "miscarriage" straight away. My GP just told me to rest and come back if it got worse.  

Did that change what I did? Yeah. I panicked and stayed home, checked things online all night. I almost went in, but I didn't. I guess I listened to the internet first, then felt stupid for it.  

Which app/site? Mostly Google and a Facebook mums group, and Flo. They felt very at odds with the GP. I handled it by reading more, crying a bit, then trying not to think about it. I'm fine. I don't know.

Did you have any pre-existing health condition before pregnancy (for example diabetes, thyroid issues, a mental-health condition, autoimmune disease, high blood pressure, or something else)? 

If so:
- What did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? (For example: review or change medications, do extra tests, offer preconception counselling, refer you to a specialist or joint clinic, give clear written plans, or something else?)
- What actually happened when you sought that help? Who did you talk to first (GP, nurse, pharmacist, specialist)? Did they give the kind of guidance you expected—timely, clear, and coordinated?
- How did those interactions make you feel? Were you listened to and supported, or did you have to push to get what you needed?
- Did anything the healthcare team did (or didn’t do) change your plans about trying to conceive?

If you didn’t have a pre-existing condition, what would you have expected healthcare services to offer someone in that situation?

Take your time — tell me as much or as little as you like. If you want, pick one specific example and walk me through what happened.

I don't have any long‑term conditions. I'm fine... I think. I don't take meds for anything, no diabetes or anything like that. I don't really know much about that stuff.

If someone did, I guess I'd expect the doctor to say what to do with meds, do some bloods, give folic‑acid advice, maybe do extra tests or send you to a specialist. Maybe some clear written stuff — I liked the idea of a simple list, not lots of big words. Or at least a sensible plan, you know?

What actually happened for me: I went to the GP once. I didn't tell them much. He said the usual — book the midwife, take folic acid, they handed me a leaflet. That was it. No real plan, no check of things I didn't even know to ask about. I didn't push. I kinda shrugged and left. Felt a bit brushed off, but I didn't want to make a fuss.

Did it change my plans about trying? I didn't plan this, so there wasn't really a "trying" plan to change. If I had known more beforehand, maybe I'd have handled things different — I don't know. Right now I'm just... taking it day by day. I probably should look stuff up myself.

Thanks — a quick follow-up: did you find yourself having to push for tests, meds or information, or did the clinic/health service proactively contact you and arrange things (calls, appointments, bloods, advice)? If you can, give an example or say how that felt.

I don't know. They didn't ring me up or anything — I had to book the GP myself. At the appointment they took bloods and told me to arrange the dating scan. They said to take folic acid; I said OK. 

I didn't push for stuff. I'm rubbish at asking questions, so I just nodded and left. Felt awkward, a bit like I was pretending it wasn't real. I'm fine.

Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

If it helps, you can tell me things like:
- Did you expect them to research things online or use fertility apps with you?
- Did you expect them to go to GP appointments, make lifestyle changes (stop smoking/drinking, take vitamins), or help track ovulation/timing?
- Were you picturing emotional support and decision‑making together, or more of a solo thing?
- What actually happened — were they more or less involved than you thought? Any surprises? How did that feel for you?

If you weren't with a partner, who did you expect would support you and what happened instead?

I don't know. I didn't expect much, really. We were shaky already. 

I thought maybe he'd read a thing or two, maybe go to a GP with me if it happened. I pictured more talking, making decisions together. Instead he...mostly shrugged. No apps, no GP, no quitting his habits. He still drinks. I didn't push.

It felt annoying and a bit lonely. But also...I don't know, part of me didn't want him in it that much. I'm fine.

It sounds like that was really disappointing and tiring for you. Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What do you think drove that—expectations, practical reasons, or something else?

Can you give an example of a specific time this happened? How did it affect how you saw him, and how did it make you feel about your own role or identity going into parenthood?

Yeah. It mostly landed on me. I don't know if that's expectations or him being lazy. Probably both.

Like—he said he'd come with me to the GP. He bailed last minute. Didn't call, didn't say why. So I went on my own, filled the forms, listened, nodded. He promised he'd read the leaflet and order the vitamins. Never did. I ended up doing all that stuff.

It made him feel smaller to me, I guess. Less... partner. More like someone who says things and doesn't follow through. And me? I feel tired just thinking about it. I don't know if I want to be the only one doing everything. But also—I do it anyway. Guess I'm good at that. I'm fine.

If you've had a previous pregnancy loss, can you tell me what you expected the healthcare system to offer when you decided to try again, and what support you actually received?

For example:
- Did you expect extra scans, tests to try to find a cause, or a preconception appointment?
- Were you offered counselling, mental‑health support, or a specialist referral?
- Which services or professionals actually helped you (GP, midwife, hospital)?
- Were there delays, gaps, or things you felt were missing?

If you haven't had a loss, it's fine to say so or to say what you would have wanted.

I haven't had a pregnancy loss. So... I haven't gone through that.

If I had, I guess I'd expect more scans, maybe tests to see why, and a proper preconception chat. Maybe someone to talk to about how it hit me — counselling or a referral. I don't know, really.

For this pregnancy I saw my GP once. It was quick. They gave a leaflet and said book with midwife. No one offered counselling or extra checks. Felt a bit... hands-off. But I'm fine.

I know you said you haven't had a pregnancy loss, so this might not apply — but if it's helpful to reflect: was there ever a point where you felt a previous loss (or the possibility of one) wasn't being taken seriously when you were trying for this pregnancy? 

If that doesn't fit, how about this instead: during your GP visit for this pregnancy, did you feel listened to and taken seriously, or were there moments you felt dismissed or not trusted?

I don't know. They were polite. I said what I had to and they did the checks. Felt a bit brushed off, like they wanted to tick boxes and move on. I didn't tell them much anyway — probably my fault. I'm fine.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people there, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

To help you answer, you can tell me about things like:
- Where the appointment was held and whether it felt welcoming or clinical
- Who was there (midwife, doctor, partner) and how involved they were
- How much information or explanation you got, and whether it matched what you hoped for
- How you felt in the appointment (listened to, rushed, anxious)
- Anything that surprised you or you wish had been different

Take your time — anything you remember is helpful.

I thought it would be… I don’t know, nicer? Like someone smiling, telling me what to do. Maybe a midwife, a cosy room, not all white walls. I imagined they'd explain things slowly. I’m bad with surprises.

It was at the GP. Small room, bright lights, plastic chairs. Felt clinical. Doctor came in. No partner. I didn’t tell anyone. They asked questions. I just shrugged a lot. I gave short answers. 

They did bloods, gave a leaflet and a letter to the hospital for a scan. Not much talk. Felt a bit rushed. I don’t think they asked how I felt about it — or I didn’t say. I felt anxious and a bit on show. 

I was surprised by the forms and how fast they moved me on. I wish someone had sat down and said the steps, slowly. I wish it felt warmer. But yeah, I’m fine. I don’t really want to think about it much.

Thank you — that helps a lot. What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

If it helps, you can tell me about:
- Anything they did that made you feel reassured or made you lose trust.
- Whether you felt listened to and respected, or rushed and ignored.
- How the room, staff tone, or explanations affected how safe or comfortable you felt.
- One small thing they could have done that would have made the experience better.

I don't know. Biggest surprise was how quick it all was. Felt like a tick‑box thing.  

One nurse smiled and said it's early and normal — that helped a bit. But the doctor mostly looked at the screen and asked questions fast. I felt rushed, like my answers didn't really matter.  

The room was cold and quiet. Staff sounded...busy, not cosy. Made me a bit on edge.  

One small thing? Just someone sitting down and asking "are you okay?" and waiting would have helped. Or a simple sheet that says what happens next.  

I'm fine.

Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — who would tell you (a midwife, doctor, sonographer?), how would they explain them (in person, on the phone, written leaflets?), and how quickly would you expect to hear? And what actually happened?

If it helps, can you say:
- Did you expect someone to sit with you and explain things slowly?
- Were you expecting written information or links to look at later?
- If a result was worrying or unclear, did you expect counselling or a follow-up appointment — and was that offered?

I thought someone would sit me down and say it all slowly. I don’t know, a midwife or the sonographer or my GP. I wanted plain words, not loads of medical talk. 

Did I expect someone to explain slowly? Yes. I expected them to. What actually happened…nobody really sat with me. If someone said anything it was quick and a bit cold. I just nodded along.

Was I expecting written info or links? Yeah. A leaflet or a link I could check later. I got a leaflet once at the GP maybe, but I didn’t read much. I’m bad at that stuff. I don’t know which website they thought I’d use.

If a result was worrying, did I expect counselling or follow-up? I thought they’d offer to talk more or book me in. That didn’t really happen. They said they’d call if anything was wrong, but I wasn’t offered a proper follow-up or counselling. I’m fine. Mostly. I don’t know.

Thank you — that’s really helpful to hear. Just to follow up: was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

If so, can you tell me a bit more about that? You might find it easier to think about answers to any of these:
- What exactly felt missing — more plain language, more detail, or just time to hear it properly?
- Did you feel rushed or like you couldn’t ask questions?
- Did anyone offer to sit with you and explain things slowly, or did staff act like the decisions were already made?
- Would you have preferred written info, a phone call later, or someone (midwife/GP/partner) with you when it was explained?
- Did the way information was given make you feel like you had control over decisions, or more like you had to rely on professionals?

Take your time — anything you can remember helps.

Yeah, there was a gap. They told me things, but it was all quick and a bit… clinical. I don't know half of what they said.

I wanted plain words. Not big medical stuff. More time would’ve helped — I felt rushed and didn’t really ask questions. No one sat with me to explain slowly. It was like they assumed I knew what to do next.

I would’ve liked a leaflet or a phone call later, or someone with me — a midwife or… I dunno, even my partner. The way it was done made me feel like I had to trust them, not like I was in control. But I’m fine.
